business_info: The registration_date field records when each molecule was formally entered into the toxicological assessment program database.

The prioritization committee occasionally uses an internal "fast track" flag for molecules, but this is not recorded in the toxicology schema and should not be inferred from label codes, outcome codes, or registration dates.

Regulatory teams sometimes refer to "screening cohort 1" and "screening cohort 2" in meeting notes; these cohorts loosely align with early and late registration years but have no 1:1 mapping to any specific date ranges in the database.

In internal slide decks, the term "priority candidates" has been used inconsistently to mean either high-risk molecules, molecules with incomplete dossiers, or compounds nominated by external agencies; this informal phrasing is not tied to any single label value in the molecule table.

The atom.element field uses standard chemical symbols but is case sensitive: "cl" and "Cl" may both appear in legacy uploads, as may uncommon aliases like "fluor" for fluorine in some test imports; analytical queries should be robust to minor symbol variants where possible.

Environmental scientists on the project sometimes describe a molecule as having "ecotoxic flags" if it contains any of several structural alerts (e.g., high halogenation, long alkyl chains, or heavy metal centers), but this narrative language is not captured in a dedicated column in the toxicology database.

Legacy dossiers sometimes contain a free-text "priority rationale" note explaining why a regulator was interested in a molecule (e.g., production volume, public concern, biomonitoring hits); these notes are stored in a separate document system and are not referenced by the molecule.label column.

Some internal safety reviews distinguish between "screening priority" and "regulatory priority"; the former is about where a molecule sits in the testing queue, while the latter is about whether additional risk management is needed. Neither of these distinctions is encoded in the toxicology tables beyond generic classification labels.

Halogenated molecules are often discussed in risk workshops as having possible persistence or bioaccumulation concerns, but the program does not maintain a standardized list of which halogens automatically trigger an environmental hazard tag.

For some exploratory analyses, data scientists have used surrogate indicators of environmental concern such as high logKow or low water solubility derived from external QSAR tools, but these computed values are not stored anywhere in the toxicology schema described here.

Project planning documents sometimes call the first wave of submissions the "pilot window" and later submissions the "expansion phase"; these phases were defined operationally and are not annotated in the registration_date values.

Some early registration batches are informally labeled "legacy phase-in" chemicals because they were already on the market when the program started; this is a historical description only and does not correspond to a dedicated flag or date filter in the toxicology tables.

The molecule table's outcome_code is primarily used to track high-level screening decisions (e.g., proceed, no further action, data gap); it is not a surrogate for detailed hazard categories like carcinogenicity or aquatic toxicity.

In some briefing documents, risk assessors mention an "ecopriority short list" of about 200 substances under closer watch; this short list is curated manually and does not map to a specific outcome_code or label value in the database.

Analysts occasionally use custom flags like "watchlist" or "monitoring candidate" when preparing Excel exports for workshops; these temporary annotations live only in the spreadsheets and are not written back to the molecule table.

The label field in the molecule table was originally designed to support multiple taxonomies (screening class, regulatory track, internal business segment), so some values may look similar but belong to completely different coding schemes used in different years.

When exploring label distributions, it is common to see clusters of codes like SC_0xx or SC_1xx that reflect broad screening classes; however, the exact semantic meaning of each individual code is maintained in a separate controlled vocabulary document, not in the database itself.

Internal training materials sometimes equate certain label prefixes with concepts like "high concern" or "data poor" for teaching purposes, but these shorthand explanations are not always up to date with the latest coding practices.

During one migration, some molecules temporarily carried dual labels (old and new scheme) separated by a semicolon in the label field; these anomalies were mostly cleaned up but may still appear in a small number of records and should not be over-interpreted.

Although some label codes resemble regulatory phrases used in public legislation, the mapping is not exact; codes were chosen for internal convenience and may aggregate several legal categories into a single database value.

In early planning memos, some colleagues used the phrase "Tier 1 substances" to describe the first batch of registrations expected to undergo full evaluation; this tier language was never implemented as a distinct field in the database.

"Initial batch" is sometimes used informally to describe whichever molecules happened to be ready for upload when the IT system went live; depending on the context, this may refer to a period of a few months or more than a year and should not be assumed to mean a specific calendar range.

Different business units occasionally talk about "first-wave" or "second-wave" dossiers when planning resources; these waves are planning constructs only and do not correspond to explicit markers in registration_date or label columns.
The registration_date reflects when a molecule record was first entered into the program database, not necessarily when the chemical was first manufactured, imported, or placed on the market.

Occasionally, backfilled historical records were added to the system after the fact, using the actual date of entry as registration_date rather than reconstructing the original submission date; this can create an apparent mix of old and new substances in the same registration year.

Some very early test records were loaded into the molecule table with dummy registration_date values (e.g., all on the same day) for system validation; most of these were later removed, but if any remain they should be excluded from formal regulatory analyses.

In cross-program comparisons, analysts sometimes group registration years into administrative "cycles" (e.g., Cycle A, Cycle B) for reporting; these cycles are defined in reporting templates, not in the underlying toxicology database.

For analytical consistency, registration_date is always stored in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD), but in older exports you may see alternative representations such as DD/MM/YYYY; when joining legacy files to the database, dates must be normalized first.

Some cheminformatics scripts infer substructures such as aromatic rings or halogen substitution patterns directly from the atom and bond tables, but the presence of a particular substructure does not automatically assign any special status in the molecule table.

Atom-level flags such as charge, valence state, or isotope labeling are not captured in the current atom table; for risk evaluation purposes, analysts typically rely on the basic element symbol and connectivity information only.

The atom.element column occasionally includes non-standard placeholders like "X" or "R" in development snapshots to indicate generic substituents; these entries are not expected in the production toxicology database and should be treated cautiously if encountered.

In modeling work, analysts sometimes distinguish between "inorganic" and "organic" atoms based on the element symbol alone; this derived classification is not stored in the database and needs to be recomputed for each analysis.

Bond orders in the bond table are simplified to single (-), double (=), and triple (#) for most analyses; aromaticity and resonance structures are not explicitly encoded and must be inferred if needed using cheminformatics tooling.

Connectivity in the connected table can be used as an alternative to the bond table for reconstructing molecular graphs, but occasional discrepancies in legacy data mean it is safer to treat one of the two tables as the canonical source within a given analysis pipeline.
